title: Discover Your Cognitive Profile
subtitle: Answer 5 quick questions and we'll score your brain across 4 key dimensions to deliver a personalised wellness plan.

fatigue.label: Fatigue
fatigue.description: Chronic mental energy depletion, brain fog, poor sleep restoration

stress.label: Stress
stress.description: Cortisol load, cognitive impairment under pressure, overwhelm

memory.label: Memory
memory.description: Recall, retention, information processing, acetylcholine pathway

focus.label: Focus
focus.description: Sustained attention, distraction resistance, deep work capacity

questions.question: What slows you down most each day?

options.label: Mental exhaustion and lack of energy
Stress, pressure, and feeling overwhelmed
Forgetting things or difficulty retaining information
Getting distracted and struggling to stay on task

questions.question: How do you typically feel by mid-afternoon?

options.label: Drained, foggy, and running on empty
Anxious and overwhelmed by what's still left to do
Trouble recalling what I worked on earlier
Scattered and jumping between tasks without finishing

questions.question: How does sleep affect your cognitive performance?

options.label: I wake up unrefreshed — sleep never seems to restore me
Racing thoughts and stress keep me from sleeping well
I sleep okay but feel mentally hazy in the morning
Sleep is fine but I lose focus throughout the day

questions.question: When facing pressure or deadlines, you tend to:

options.label: Feel completely depleted with no mental reserves
Become anxious and struggle to make clear decisions
Go blank and forget things you normally know well
Become scattered and unable to concentrate on one thing

questions.question: Which improvement would make the biggest difference in your life?

options.label: Waking up energised with sustained mental clarity all day
Feeling calm and resilient under pressure
Reliable memory and sharp information recall
Deep, uninterrupted focus and resistance to distraction

fatigue.title: Your Primary Challenge: Mental Fatigue
fatigue.description: Your answers indicate chronic mental energy depletion is your biggest barrier. Brain fog and poor sleep restoration are draining your cognitive reserves. Here are evidence-based strategies to rebuild your mental energy.
tips: Optimise sleep restoration — maintain a consistent bedtime and wakeup time every day. Your brain needs predictable sleep cycles to fully restore cognitive function.
Address sleep quality, not just quantity — avoid screens for 60 minutes before bed, keep your room cool (around 18°C), and try magnesium glycinate to deepen sleep stages.
Eat for sustained energy — combine protein, healthy fats, and complex carbs at every meal to prevent the blood sugar crashes that trigger afternoon brain fog.
Take strategic movement breaks — a 5-minute walk every 60–90 minutes significantly increases cerebral blood flow and clears mental fatigue far better than caffeine.
Try cold exposure in the morning — a 30-second cold finish to your shower triggers a noradrenaline surge that can sustain alertness and energy for 2–4 hours.

stress.title: Your Primary Challenge: Cognitive Stress Load
stress.description: Your profile shows elevated cortisol impact on your thinking — stress is impairing your decision-making and mental clarity. These strategies target your body's stress-response system directly.
tips: Practise physiological sighing — a double inhale through the nose followed by a long exhale is the fastest known way to reduce acute cortisol and calm the nervous system.
Schedule a daily decompression window — even 15 minutes of deliberate rest (walking, reading, music) after high-demand periods prevents cortisol from accumulating.
Limit caffeine after midday — caffeine amplifies cortisol output and disrupts the deep sleep your brain needs to recover from stress.
Use the 5-4-3-2-1 grounding method when overwhelmed — name 5 things you can see, 4 you can touch, 3 you can hear. This anchors your prefrontal cortex and overrides fight-or-flight.
Prioritise social connection — even brief, positive social interaction reduces cortisol. A short check-in with a friend or colleague can shift your stress chemistry significantly.

memory.title: Your Primary Challenge: Memory & Retention
memory.description: Your results highlight the memory and recall dimension — the acetylcholine pathway plays a key role here. These science-backed techniques will help you retain and retrieve information more effectively.
tips: Use active recall, not passive re-reading — close your notes and try to retrieve information from scratch. This strengthens memory consolidation far more effectively than reviewing.
Apply spaced repetition — review new material at intervals of 1 day, 3 days, 7 days, then 21 days. Each review moves information deeper into long-term storage.
Prioritise sleep for memory consolidation — your brain processes and cements memories during deep slow-wave sleep. Even one night of poor sleep can reduce next-day recall by up to 40%.
Reduce multitasking — it takes about 8 seconds of focused attention to form a new memory. Constant task-switching prevents proper encoding.
Eat for acetylcholine — eggs, fish, nuts, and leafy greens are rich in choline, the precursor to acetylcholine, the neurotransmitter most critical to memory formation.

focus.title: Your Primary Challenge: Sustained Focus
focus.description: Your profile points to difficulty with sustained attention and distraction resistance. Deep work capacity is a trainable skill — these strategies will help you build it systematically.
tips: Use time-blocking with commitment devices — schedule focus blocks in your calendar and silence all notifications during them. Treat these blocks as non-negotiable appointments.
Try the Pomodoro Technique — 25 minutes of focused work followed by a 5-minute break. After 4 cycles, take a 20-minute break. This trains your attention span like a muscle.
Identify and eliminate your top distraction trigger — whether it's your phone, email, or background noise, removing the single biggest trigger can improve focus duration by 50%+.
Exercise before deep work — 20 minutes of moderate aerobic exercise boosts dopamine and norepinephrine levels for up to 2 hours, directly enhancing sustained attention.
Start with your hardest task first — willpower and focus are finite resources that deplete through the day. Do your most demanding work within the first 90 minutes of waking.
